---
title: "Module 5: Scientific Writing in R Markdown"
subtitle: "Equations, Citations, Cross-References, and Parameters"
author: "NIEHS Training"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: true
    code_folding: show
params:
  chemical: "PFOA"
  threshold: 4.0
  study_year: 2024
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
```

# LaTeX Equations

R Markdown supports LaTeX math notation, which renders beautifully in HTML and
PDF output.

## Inline Equations

Surround with single dollar signs: `$E = mc^2$` renders as $E = mc^2$.

More examples:

| Syntax | Renders as |
|--------|-----------|
| `$\alpha = 0.05$` | $\alpha = 0.05$ |
| `$R^2 = 0.87$` | $R^2 = 0.87$ |
| `$\beta_1 = 0.42$` | $\beta_1 = 0.42$ |
| `$p < 0.001$` | $p < 0.001$ |
| `$\mu \pm \sigma$` | $\mu \pm \sigma$ |
| `$\text{BMI} = \frac{kg}{m^2}$` | $\text{BMI} = \frac{kg}{m^2}$ |
| `$\log_{10}(\text{PFOA})$` | $\log_{10}(\text{PFOA})$ |

## Display Equations

Surround with double dollar signs for centered, display-style equations:

```
$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \beta_2 X_{i2} + \epsilon_i$$
```

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \beta_2 X_{i2} + \epsilon_i$$

A dose-response model:

$$\text{Response} = \frac{E_{\max} \cdot C^n}{EC_{50}^n + C^n}$$

Where $E_{\max}$ is the maximum effect, $C$ is concentration, $EC_{50}$ is the
half-maximal effective concentration, and $n$ is the Hill coefficient.

## Common Patterns for Environmental Health

```
$$\text{HQ} = \frac{\text{CDI}}{\text{RfD}}$$
```

$$\text{HQ} = \frac{\text{CDI}}{\text{RfD}}$$

Where HQ is the hazard quotient, CDI is the chronic daily intake, and RfD is
the reference dose.

```
$$\text{LADD} = \frac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT}$$
```

$$\text{LADD} = \frac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT}$$


# Parameterized Reports

One of R Markdown's most powerful features is **parameters**. You define
parameters in the YAML header, and they become variables you can use anywhere
in the document.

## How Parameters Work

Look at the YAML header of this file — it includes:

````
params:
  chemical: "PFOA"
  threshold: 4.0
  study_year: 2024
````

You access parameters with `params$chemical`, `params$threshold`, etc.

## Using Parameters

This report analyzes **`r params$chemical`** data from **`r params$study_year`**
using a threshold of **`r params$threshold` ng/mL**.

```{r param-demo}
# Generate data based on parameter
set.seed(42)
exposure_data <- data.frame(
  participant = 1:100,
  concentration = round(rlnorm(100, meanlog = 0.8, sdlog = 0.9), 2)
)

n_above <- sum(exposure_data$concentration > params$threshold)
pct_above <- round(n_above / nrow(exposure_data) * 100, 1)
```

Of the `r nrow(exposure_data)` participants, **`r n_above` (`r pct_above`%)**
had serum `r params$chemical` concentrations exceeding the
`r params$threshold` ng/mL threshold.

```{r param-plot, fig.width=7, fig.height=4, echo=FALSE}
ggplot(exposure_data, aes(x = concentration)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "#0D9488", color = "white", bins = 25) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = params$threshold, color = "#DC2626", 
             linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = params$threshold + 0.5, y = Inf, 
           label = paste("Threshold:", params$threshold, "ng/mL"),
           vjust = 2, hjust = 0, color = "#DC2626", size = 3.5) +
  labs(title = paste(params$chemical, "Serum Concentrations"),
       x = paste(params$chemical, "(ng/mL)"),
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Re-Rendering with Different Parameters

You can knit the same report with different parameter values:

```{r rerender, eval=FALSE}
# Render for PFOS instead of PFOA, with a different threshold
rmarkdown::render(
  "05_scientific_writing.Rmd",
  params = list(chemical = "PFOS", threshold = 6.0, study_year = 2025),
  output_file = "report_PFOS_2025.html"
)
```

This means one `.Rmd` file can generate reports for every chemical, every year,
every site — automatically. Imagine generating 50 site reports from a single
template!

## The "Knit with Parameters" Button

In RStudio, click the dropdown arrow next to the **Knit** button and select
**"Knit with Parameters..."** to get a dialog where you can fill in parameter
values interactively.


# Tabbed Sections {.tabset}

In HTML output, you can organize content into tabs by adding `{.tabset}` to a
heading. This is useful for showing related but alternative content.

## PFAS Overview

PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) are a group of manufactured
chemicals used since the 1940s in various industrial and consumer products.

## Exposure Routes

Common exposure routes include contaminated drinking water, food packaging,
and occupational exposure in manufacturing facilities.

## Health Effects

NIEHS-funded research has linked PFAS exposure to immune system effects,
thyroid disease, liver damage, and certain cancers.

# {.unnumbered .unlisted}

(The tabs above demonstrate the `.tabset` feature.)


# Callout Blocks and Special Formatting

While R Markdown doesn't have built-in callout boxes, you can create them with
simple HTML or use custom CSS:

<div style="padding: 12px; background-color: #F0FDF4; border-left: 4px solid #0D9488; margin: 16px 0;">
**Tip:** When writing a methods section, describe the statistical analysis in
enough detail that another researcher could replicate it from your report alone.
</div>

<div style="padding: 12px; background-color: #FEF3C7; border-left: 4px solid #D97706; margin: 16px 0;">
**Warning:** Never include personally identifiable information (PII) or
protected health information (PHI) in R Markdown documents that will be shared
or committed to version control.
</div>


# Images from Files

To include an external image (e.g., a diagram, a microscopy photo, a logo):

**Markdown syntax:**

```
![Caption text](path/to/image.png)
```

**R chunk syntax** (gives you more control over size):

````
```{r, out.width="50%", fig.cap="Figure: Exposure pathway diagram"}`r ''`
knitr::include_graphics("path/to/image.png")
```
````

The chunk method is preferred because you can use `out.width`, `fig.align`,
`fig.cap`, and other chunk options to control the appearance.


# Bibliographies and Citations

R Markdown supports proper academic citations using a `.bib` file.

## Setting Up Citations

1. Create a BibTeX file (e.g., `references.bib`) with your references
2. Point to it in the YAML header

````yaml
---
title: "My Report"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
---
````

A BibTeX entry looks like this:

````
@article{smith2023pfas,
  title = {PFAS Exposure and Health Outcomes},
  author = {Smith, Jane and Doe, John},
  journal = {Environmental Health Perspectives},
  year = {2023},
  volume = {131},
  pages = {1--12},
  doi = {10.1289/EHP12345}
}
````

## Citing in Text

- `@smith2023pfas` → Smith and Doe (2023)
- `[@smith2023pfas]` → (Smith and Doe, 2023)
- `[@smith2023pfas; @jones2024bpa]` → (Smith and Doe, 2023; Jones, 2024)
- `[-@smith2023pfas]` → (2023) — suppress author name

A **References** section is automatically generated at the end of the document.

## Citation Style

Download a CSL (Citation Style Language) file for your target journal from
[zotero.org/styles](https://www.zotero.org/styles) and specify it in the YAML:

````yaml
csl: environmental-health-perspectives.csl
````

> **Practical tip for this workshop:** We won't create a `.bib` file today,
> but knowing this system exists means you can adopt it for manuscripts. Tools
> like Zotero can export `.bib` files directly.


# Exercise 5.1: Write an Equation and Use Parameters {.unnumbered}

**Hands-on practice (5 minutes):**

1. Write the equation for the **odds ratio** using LaTeX display math:
   $OR = \frac{a/b}{c/d}$ (make it display-style with `$$`)
2. In this file's YAML, change `chemical` to `"PFOS"` and `threshold` to `6.0`
3. Re-knit and observe how the text, plot, and statistics all update

---

**Next up:** `06_output_formats.Rmd` — HTML themes, Word, PDF, and more.
